answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's closing chapter (Ch. 19 of *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. II) is more programmatic than Charles Comte's (Ch. 27 of *Traité de Législation*, Vol. II).**[^1][^2]

Comte's chapter is ethnographic and descriptive, analyzing the social hierarchies, feudal structures, slavery, justice systems (e.g., poison/fire ordeals), and morals among "negro species" peoples on Africa's western tropical coasts. It draws parallels to Malay and other "species" (e.g., more organization than southern African hunter-gatherers due to agriculture), emphasizing conquest-based inequalities and aristocratic idleness—but offers no prescriptions, reforms, or forward agenda. It culminates in a brief comparison of security/fertility advantages over savages, then simply states "#### END OF THE SECOND VOLUME."[^3] The tone is observational, fitting Vol. II's broader racial/climatic surveys, with no call to action.[^1]

Dunoyer's, by contrast, is a sustained policy blueprint for liberating "arts that work on the education of our intellectual faculties." He critiques classical-language monopoly (e.g., Greek/Latin as inferior to modern tongues/practical knowledge), urges alignment of teaching with industrial "arts" (e.g., via cerebral gymnastics, mutual instruction, specialization, machines like presses/telegraphs), demands civil liberties (no state monopolies on schools/printing), and extols moral reforms (sobriety, anti-luxury). Statistics on exploding schools/papers/readership prove progress under freer conditions, projecting societal ascent via intellectual capitalism: "ideas provoke ideas; books beget books." It ends on triumphant metrics before "#### END OF THE SECOND VOLUME."—a veritable manifesto for educational industrialization.[^2]

Side-by-side:

| Aspect                  | Comte (Ch. 27)                                                                 | Dunoyer (Ch. 19)                                                                 |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Core Content**       | Descriptive anthropology: feudal kings/vassals/slaves, gender roles, ordeals. | Prescriptive economics: free education industries, methods, anti-monopoly.       |
| **Forward Orientation**| None; empirical close on African morals vs. Cape peoples.                      | Strong: reforms for brain-training, press liberty, progress stats.               |
| **Program Elements**   | Implicit critique of barbarism via comparisons.                                | Explicit: business talent, division of labor, machines, habits for educators.    |
| **Ending Signal**      | Abrupt volume end after comparison.[^3]                                        | Optimistic data on idea-multiplication, volume end.[^4]                          |

Dunoyer's integrates theory-into-agenda (e.g., §4–9 on enterprise, stocks, civil habits), while Comte's stays analytical—making Dunoyer's the clearer "program."

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, Ch. 27 (/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/27-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/).
[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Ch. 19 (/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/19-ix-de-la-liberte-des-arts-qui-travaillent-a-leduca/translation/).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-f35d7e] (/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/27-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-f35d7e).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-96e79d–p-a58997] (/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/19-ix-de-la-liberte-des-arts-qui-travaillent-a-leduca/translation/#p-96e79d).
